_text:
anna: Anna,
john: yesterday I destroyed a meeting.
anna: Destroyed?
anna: That is a strong word.
john: Okay, maybe not destroyed, but I did say something very unforgettable.
anna: Now I am worried.
john: I said, we need to improve the customer potato.
anna: The customer potato?
john: Portal.
john: I meant portal.
anna: That is a very different business strategy.
john: Very different.
john: Very agricultural.
anna: Okay, back up.
anna: What actually happened?
john: Someone asked for my opinion.
john: And the thing is, I knew my idea.
john: I had it clearly in my head.
anna: Right.
john: But the second everyone looked at me, my brain got nervous and chose the wrong word.
anna: Ah, the pressure moment.
john: Exactly.
john: My English was not the problem.
john: My confidence left the room for a second.
anna: That happens to so many people.
john: So instead of explaining my idea calmly, I started speaking faster, using bigger words and smiling like a business robot.
anna: Very professional.
john: Very nervous.
anna: Slightly overheating.
john: You get it now.
anna: And after the… potato incident?
john: I apologized three times and said, sorry, I am bad at explaining, even though my idea was actually clear in my head.
john: I just got nervous.
anna: That is such a real moment.
anna: Sometimes the problem is not your English, it is the pressure of building your answer clearly in the moment.

anna: Sometimes the problem is not your English, it is the pressure of building your answer clearly in the moment.
john: Yes, I had the idea, but I did not have a calm path for saying it.
anna: And that is exactly why we created the English Leap Answer Builder.
john: Because many learners understand the question.
anna: They know what they want to say.
john: But when it is time to answer, the answer comes out too short, too fast, or too messy.
anna: The Answer Builder helps you build fuller answers step by step with a reason, a feeling, an example, and a personal detail.
john: So your English sounds more natural, more confident, and more like you.
anna: It is included inside English Leap Club, and you can also get it separately.
anna: The link is below in the description.
john: So today, we fix my customer portal.
anna: Not potato.
john: Not potato.
anna: Not potato.
john: And we help you communicate with calm, clear confidence.
anna: Hey English learners, welcome back to the English Leap Podcast, your cozy little place to learn easy English through real, everyday conversations.
anna: I am Anna.
john: And I am Jake, currently recovering from yesterday's meeting.
anna: Today we are talking about how to communicate with confidence in English.
john: Not fake confidence.
anna: Not speak fast and hope everyone understands.
john: I have tried that method.
john: It is not safe.

john: Not fake confidence.
anna: Not speak fast and hope everyone understands.
john: I have tried that method.
john: It is not safe.
anna: We are talking about real confidence, the kind that helps you stay in the conversation even when you feel nervous.
john: And later in Say It Better, we will take nervous learner sentences and make them sound more confident, natural, and kind.
anna: And in Word Tour, we will slow down some useful B2 to C1 words for this topic.
anna: Alright, let us start with the big mistake many learners make.
john: Thinking confidence means speaking fast.
anna: Yes, and honestly Jake, learners are not the only ones who believe that.
john: Okay, but I need to admit something before we go further.
anna: Go on, I am ready.
john: When I hear confident English speakers, they sound fast, they do not stop, they do not search for words, so I thought if I speak faster, I will sound more fluent.
anna: I understand why you thought that.
john: That sounds like you are about to disagree politely.
anna: I am.
john: I knew it!
anna: Fast English can sound impressive for about five seconds.
john: Only five?
anna: Maybe seven if the listener is kind.
john: Painful but fair.
anna: But after that, the question is not, was it fast?
anna: The question is, was it clear?
john: Clear means easy to understand.
anna: Yes, and today that matters more than speed.

anna: The question is, was it clear?
john: Clear means easy to understand.
anna: Yes, and today that matters more than speed.
john: But fast speakers sound confident.
anna: Some do, but some fast speakers are just nervous in a hurry.
john: Nervous in a hurry, that was me.
anna: And some slower speakers sound very confident because they are calm, clear, and in control.
john: So if I slow down, people will not think I am weak?
anna: Not if you speak with purpose.
john: With purpose?
anna: Yes.
anna: There is a difference between freezing and pausing.
anna: Hmm.
john: Explain.
anna: Freezing feels like your mind has disappeared.
john: Familiar.
anna: Pausing means you take a short moment to choose your next thought.
john: So a pause can be confident?
anna: It can be very confident.
john: I do not know.
john: In my head, silence feels dangerous.
anna: That is self-doubt talking.
john: The inner voice?
anna: Yes.
anna: The voice that says, they are judging you, you sound slow, hurry.
john: Mine says all of that with excellent pronunciation.
anna: Mine too, sometimes.
john: Really?
anna: Of course.
anna: Everyone has moments of self-doubt.
john: Even confident speakers?
anna: Especially confident speakers.
anna: They just know how to manage it.
john: That is comforting.
anna: Good, because confidence does not mean you never feel nervous.
john: Then what does it mean?
anna: It means you can stay composed while you speak.
john: Composed.
john: Calm, but not frozen.
anna: Nice.
anna: That is a good way to say it.

john: Composed.
john: Calm, but not frozen.
anna: Nice.
anna: That is a good way to say it.
john: So, if I forget a word but I do not panic, I am composed.
john: Yes.
john: If someone asks me a question and I take a breath before answering, I am composed.
anna: Yes.
john: If I say, customer potato, and do not leave the country, I am composed.
anna: That might be advanced composed.
john: Good.
john: I need that level.
anna: Let us look at what happened in your meeting.
john: Do we have to?
anna: Gently.
john: Fine.
anna: You had an idea.
john: Yes.
anna: Then everyone looked at you.
john: Terrible moment.
anna: You felt pressure.
john: A lot.
anna: So you rushed.
john: Yes.
anna: And because you rushed, your words became less clear.
john: That is what happened.
anna: Then you made one mistake.
john: One unforgettable mistake.
anna: And after the mistake, you apologize too much.
john: I did.
anna: That is the part we need to change.
john: Not the mistake?
anna: Mistakes will happen.
anna: We can reduce them with practice, but we cannot remove them from life.
john: I dislike that answer.
anna: I know.
anna: But it is true.
john: So the real skill is not never making a mistake.
anna: The real skill is recovering after a mistake.
john: Recovering means getting control again.
anna: Yes.
anna: When you recover, you do not let one mistake destroy the whole conversation.
john: That is hard.

anna: Yes.
anna: When you recover, you do not let one mistake destroy the whole conversation.
john: That is hard.
anna: It is.
anna: But it is learnable.
john: Give me a better version of what I should have done.
anna: Okay.
anna: You say, we need to improve the customer potato.
john: Unfortunately.
anna: Then, instead of panicking, you pause.
john: Like that?
anna: Maybe a little shorter.
john: Good note.
anna: Then you say, sorry, I mean customer portal.
john: That is it?
john: That is it.
john: No speech about how bad I am at explaining?
anna: No.
john: I needed to hear that.
anna: One small correction is enough.
john: Sorry, I mean customer portal.
anna: Or let me correct that, customer portal.
john: That sounds much more confident.
anna: Because it keeps the focus on the message, not on your fear.
john: Ah, when I say, I am bad at explaining, I move attention to what went wrong.
anna: That is close.
anna: I would say you move attention away from your idea.
john: Right.
john: The idea disappears and now everyone is thinking about the mistake.
anna: Maybe not everyone, but you make the mistake the topic.
john: And I do not want that.
anna: No.
anna: You want the idea to stay the topic.
john: So confident communication protects the message.
anna: That is a beautiful line.
john: Thank you.
john: I am healing.
anna: Slowly, but yes.
john: Okay, so confidence is not speaking fast.
john: It is speaking clearly.

john: Thank you.
john: I am healing.
anna: Slowly, but yes.
john: Okay, so confidence is not speaking fast.
john: It is speaking clearly.
john: Good.
john: A pause is not failure.
john: A pause can show control.
john: Strong.
john: And if I make a mistake, I recover instead of attacking myself.
anna: That one matters most.
john: Are we done?
john: I feel emotionally repaired.
anna: Not yet.
john: I feared that.
anna: There is another confidence skill learners often ignore.
john: What?
anna: Asking for clarification.
john: Oh, I hate that.
john: Why?
john: Because if I ask someone to repeat something, I feel like they will think my English is weak.
anna: Hmm.
anna: Or they may think you are actually listening.
john: That is not where I thought you were going.
anna: Asking for clarification means asking someone to make something clearer.
john: Clarify.
john: Make it clearer.
anna: Yes, and confident speakers clarify all the time.
john: Native speakers too?
anna: Jake, native speakers say, sorry, what do you mean?
anna: Every day.
john: That is true.
anna: They say, can you explain that another way?
anna: They say, I did not catch the last part.
anna: They say, just to be clear, do you mean this or that?
john: But when English learners say it, they feel embarrassed.
anna: Because they think understanding everything immediately is proof of confidence.
john: Is not it?
anna: No.
anna: It is proof that you understood that moment.
anna: That is all.
john: Hmm.
john: That is annoyingly reasonable.

john: Is not it?
anna: No.
anna: It is proof that you understood that moment.
anna: That is all.
john: Hmm.
john: That is annoyingly reasonable.
anna: Real confidence is not pretending you understood.
john: Oof.
anna: Real confidence is caring enough to check.
john: So if I do not understand something in a meeting, I should not just smile and hope the topic disappears?
anna: Please do not.
john: I have done that.
anna: Many learners have.
john: Someone says, can you handle that by Friday?
john: And you smile because you understood Friday.
anna: Dangerous.
john: Very dangerous.
john: Then Friday becomes a surprise.
anna: A confident phrase would be, sorry, could you repeat the action point?
john: That sounds professional.
anna: Or just to clarify, do you want me to send the report by Friday?
john: That is much better than smiling like a confused lamp.
anna: A confused lamp?
john: Bright outside, empty inside.
anna: Strange, but useful.
john: Thank you.
anna: Let us make this practical without making it complicated.
anna: Please.
anna: You need three kinds of phrases.
john: Okay.
anna: One phrase to give yourself time.
john: Let me think
john: for a second.
john: Good.
john: Or that is a good question.
john: Let me organize my thoughts.
anna: Better for a meeting.
john: Nice.
anna: One phrase to clarify.
john: Sorry, could you say that again?
anna: Useful.
john: Or just to clarify, do you mean?
anna: Very useful.
john: And one phrase to recover.
john: Yes.
john: Let me say that again.
john: Good.
john: I used the wrong word.

anna: Very useful.
john: And one phrase to recover.
john: Yes.
john: Let me say that again.
john: Good.
john: I used the wrong word.
anna: I mean Even better.
john: Let me explain it another way.
anna: That one is gold.
john: So I do not need twenty phrases.
anna: No.
anna: Start with three.
anna: One to pause, one to clarify, one to recover.
john: That feels possible.
anna: It is.
anna: And it gives your brain something to hold when you feel nervous.
john: I like that.
john: A phrase can be like a handle.
anna: Yes.
anna: When the conversation feels slippery, you have something to hold.
john: That image works.
anna: Good.
anna: I was not sure for a second.
john: Very human of you.
anna: Thank you.
john: But let me push back.
anna: Please.
john: What if people really do judge my English?
anna: Some people might.
john: That was not comforting.
anna: I know.
anna: But pretending nobody ever judges anyone would not be honest.
anna: True.
anna: Some people may notice your mistake.
anna: Some people may be impatient.
anna: Some people may not understand your accent immediately.
john: So now I feel worse.
anna: Stay with me.
john: I
john: am staying.
anna: You cannot control every listener.
anna: No.
anna: You can control your response.
anna: Meaning?
anna: You can slow down.
anna: You can clarify.
anna: You can recover.
anna: You can keep your message clear.
anna: You can avoid attacking yourself.
john: So, confidence is not controlling what everyone thinks?
anna: No.
anna: That would be impossible.
john: Confidence is controlling how I continue.

john: So, confidence is not controlling what everyone thinks?
anna: No.
anna: That would be impossible.
john: Confidence is controlling how I continue.
anna: That is the heart of it.
john: So, if I make a mistake and say, sorry, my English is bad, I give the mistake too much power.
anna: Better.
anna: You give the mistake more attention than the message.
john: But if I say, let me correct that, I continue.
anna: And you protect the conversation.
john: I am starting to understand.
anna: Good.
john: So, what should English learners practice this week?
anna: Three simple things.
john: Always three.
anna: Three is friendly.
john: Go on.
anna: First, practice one pause phrase.
john: Like, let me think for a second.
anna: Say it out loud until it feels ready in your mouth.
john: Ready in your mouth.
john: I like that.
anna: Because when you are nervous, you do not want to invent every sentence from zero.
john: You want a phrase ready.
anna: Right.
john: Second?
anna: Practice one clarification phrase.
john: Just to clarify, do you mean Good.
anna: That phrase can save you from many misunderstandings.
john: Because I am checking before I continue.
john: Yes.
anna: Third?
anna: Practice one recovery phrase.
john: Let me say that again.
anna: Good.
john: What I mean is Also good.
john: I used the wrong word.
john: I mean Very useful.
john: So instead of preparing to be perfect, I prepared to continue.
anna: That is the shift.
john: That is much better.
anna: Because confidence does not appear before you speak.

anna: That is the shift.
john: That is much better.
anna: Because confidence does not appear before you speak.
john: Wait, say that again?
anna: Confidence does not appear before you speak.
anna: It grows because you speak.
anna: Pause, clarify, recover, and continue.
john: That feels like the sentence learners should remember.
anna: I think so, too.
john: So, today, learners do not need perfect English.
john: No.
john: They need one phrase to pause, one phrase to clarify, and one phrase to recover.
anna: That is a strong start.
john: And if something goes wrong?
anna: You continue.
john: If the word disappears,
anna: you explain another way.
john: If the listener looks confused, you clarify.
john: If the sentence comes out messy, you try again.
john: I like this version of confidence.
anna: Me too.
john: Now that we have used some strong words, should we slow them down?
anna: Yes, let us go to Word Tour.
anna: Now it is time for Word Tour, where we slow down strong words from today's conversation.
john: These words will help you talk about confidence, communication, mistakes, and staying calm while speaking English.
anna: Our first word is clarity.
john: Clarity.
anna: Clarity means being easy to understand.
john: So, if I speak clearly and people can follow my idea, I have clarity.
anna: You can say, I want to speak with more clarity in meetings.
john: Or, speaking fast is not useful if there is no clarity.

john: Or, speaking fast is not useful if there is no clarity.
anna: That sentence may be useful for you personally.
john: Gently attacked, but accepted.
anna: Not attacked, supported.
john: Even better.
john: Our second word is composed.
anna: Composed.
anna: Composed.
john: Composed means calm and in control, especially in a difficult situation.
anna: If you forget a word but take a breath and continue, you are staying composed.
john: You can say, I tried to stay composed during the interview.
anna: Or, she sounded composed even when the question was difficult.
john: I like this word.
john: It sounds calm.
anna: It does.
anna: Just do not use it to pretend you never feel nervous.
john: So composed is not emotionless.
anna: No.
anna: It means you feel the pressure, but you do not let it drive the car.
john: That is useful.
anna: Our third word is clarify.
john: Clarify.
anna: To clarify means to make something clearer or ask someone to make something clearer.
john: If I do not understand, I can say, could you clarify what you mean?
anna: Or, just to clarify, do you need this today or tomorrow?
john: That sounds professional.
anna: It is.
anna: Clarifying is not weakness.
john: It shows you care about understanding.
anna: This is one of the best confidence skills.
john: Because pretending is risky.
anna: Very risky.
john: Our fourth word is hesitate.
anna: Hesitate.
anna: Hesitate.
john: To hesitate means to pause because you feel unsure or nervous.

anna: Very risky.
john: Our fourth word is hesitate.
anna: Hesitate.
anna: Hesitate.
john: To hesitate means to pause because you feel unsure or nervous.
anna: Many learners hesitate before speaking because they are afraid of making mistakes.
john: You can say, I hesitate when I have to speak in a meeting.
anna: Or, do not hesitate to ask for clarification.
john: That phrase is very common.
anna: Yes, it means please feel free to ask.
john: But there is a difference between hesitating forever and taking a useful pause.
anna: Great point.
anna: A useful pause helps you continue.
anna: Hesitating forever keeps you silent.
john: That difference matters.
anna: Our fifth word is recover.
john: Re-co-ver.
john: Recover.
anna: To recover means to get control again after a mistake, problem, or difficult moment.
john: If I forget a word, pause, and explain it another way, I recover.
anna: You can say, I made a mistake, but I recovered and continued.
john: Or, learning how to recover helps me speak with more confidence.
anna: That is one of the most important ideas today.
john: Confidence is not never falling.
john: It is knowing how to stand again.
anna: Beautiful.
anna: Keep that.
john: I will.
john: Our sixth phrase is self-doubt.
anna: Self-doubt.
anna: Self-doubt.
john: Self-doubt is the feeling that makes you question yourself or your ability.
anna: Self-doubt says, my English is not good enough, or everyone will judge me.
john: You can say, self-doubt makes me hesitate before I speak.

john: You can say, self-doubt makes me hesitate before I speak.
anna: Or, I am learning to manage my self-doubt and keep speaking.
john: Manage is important there.
anna: Yes.
anna: We do not always remove self-doubt completely, but we can manage it.
john: That feels honest.
anna: So those are today's strong words.
anna: Clarity, composed, clarify, hesitate, recover, and self-doubt.
john: And these words are not just for listening.
anna: Right.
anna: Choose one or two and use them in your own sentences this week.
john: That is also why we have the English Leap Club, with transcripts, word tour practice, quizzes, useful vocabulary, and shadowing practice.
anna: It helps you come back to the episode and practice more slowly.
john: Because hearing a phrase once is helpful.
anna: But using it again is where confidence starts to grow.
john: And now, let us use some of today's ideas in more natural sentences.
anna: Yes, it is time for Say It Better, where we take simple learner sentences and make them sound more natural, confident, and kind.
john: Today we are upgrading the sentences learners say when they feel nervous about speaking.
anna: Here is the first one.
anna: Sorry, my English is bad.
john: I have said this.
anna: Many learners have.
anna: But this sentence makes your English the problem before people even hear your idea.
john: A better sentence is, I am still improving, but let me try.

john: A better sentence is, I am still improving, but let me try.
anna: Better, but I would make it even more confident in a meeting.
john: How?
anna: I may need a moment, but I would like to share my idea.
john: That sounds calm.
anna: Yes, it gives you space without attacking your English.
john: So, I do not need to apologize before I speak.
anna: Not for having an accent.
anna: Not for being a learner.
anna: Not for trying.
john: Here is another one.
john: I do not know how to say.
anna: The meaning is clear, but it sounds unfinished.
john: A better sentence is, I am looking for the right word.
john: Good.
john: It tells the listener what is happening.
anna: And if the word still does not come?
john: Let me explain it another way.
anna: Perfect.
anna: That is the real confidence move.
john: Explain around the word.
anna: Yes, do not stop just because one word is missing.
anna: Here is another one.
anna: You understand me.
john: That sentence is understandable, but it can sound a little too direct.
anna: A better sentence is, does that make sense?
john: Very natural.
anna: Or, am I explaining that clearly?
john: That sounds thoughtful.
anna: Yes, but use it honestly.
anna: Do not ask every ten seconds.
john: Ah, too much checking can sound nervous.
anna: Right.
anna: Use it when you really want to check the message.

john: Ah, too much checking can sound nervous.
anna: Right.
anna: Use it when you really want to check the message.
john: Here is another one.
john: Repeat again.
anna: The meaning is clear, but it can sound too direct.
john: A better sentence is, could you say that again, please?
anna: Good.
john: Or, sorry, I did not quite catch that.
anna: Very natural.
john: That sounds confident because I am not pretending.
anna: Yes, but in a professional situation, keep your tone calm.
john: So not panic voice.
anna: Not panic voice.
anna: Noted.
anna: Here is another one.
anna: I made a mistake.
john: We need a small grammar fix first.
john: I made a mistake.
anna: Yes, but in conversation you may not even need to announce the mistake.
anna: Really?
anna: Sometimes just correct it and continue.
john: A better sentence is, let me correct that.
john: Good.
john: Or, I used the wrong word.
john: I mean
anna: That helps you recover quickly.
john: And it keeps the conversation moving.
anna: That is the goal.
john: Here is the last one.
john: I cannot speak confidently.
anna: That sentence sounds too final.
john: It closes the door.
anna: A better sentence is, I am learning to stay calm while I speak.
john: I like that.
anna: It focuses on the skill you are building.
john: You could also say, I am practicing how to pause, clarify, and recover.
anna: That is stronger because it gives you a plan.
john: A plan is better than a label.

anna: That is stronger because it gives you a plan.
john: A plan is better than a label.
anna: Yes, I cannot speak confidently is a label.
anna: I am practicing how to pause, clarify, and recover is a direction.
john: That feels much better.
anna: Before we finish, remember this.
anna: Confident English does not have to be perfect English.
john: Confidence is not controlling what everyone thinks.
anna: It is controlling how you continue.
john: Speak clearly.
anna: Take a pause.
john: Ask for clarification.
anna: Recover when something goes wrong.
john: And do not make your English smaller than your idea.
anna: Choose one sentence today.
anna: One pause phrase, one clarification phrase, or one recovery phrase.
john: Practice it until it feels ready in your mouth.
anna: Because you are not preparing to be perfect.
john: You are preparing to continue.
anna: That is all for today.
john: See you in the next episode.
anna: Bye for now.
anna: Your progress does not end here.
anna: To continue advancing your English skills, click on the next video or explore the additional videos we have thoughtfully selected for you.
